title: ABC谋杀案
summary:
During an interrogation, Alexander Bonaparte Castor defends himself against accusations regarding the ABC murders. He maintains that the letters received were part of a conspiracy and claims that the ABC train timetables found in his closet were mistaken for his stock of stockings. When questioned about a list where Mrs. Ascher's name had been crossed out, Castor asserts he simply decided to start with her. He attempts to prove his innocence regarding the Bexhill murder by stating he was playing dominoes in Eastbourne at the time.

Castor recounts a somber memory of a man he met while playing dominoes who claimed that fate is written on one's hand. This man predicted that Castor would become famous but would die a violent death on the gallows. Following this recollection, Castor suffers a headache and collapses. Upon regaining a calm state, he admits to Poirot that he killed someone, though he confesses he does not know why he did it.

In his final case analysis, Hercule Poirot explains that the case was an "ABC mystery" centered on the killer's psyche rather than a traditional motive. Poirot notes that while the murders followed an alphabetical pattern, the central puzzle was the killer's decision to write specifically to him rather than to the police or newspapers. Poirot rejects theories of simple inferiority or bloodlust, noting that a killer driven by bloodlust would hide their tracks rather than advertise them. He concludes that the killer used the ABC timetables as part of an obsessive pattern and chose victims specifically to avoid suspicion.
